items.prompt_md: In the classic binary search, once you see `nums[mid] < target`, what do you know?
options: The target cannot be in the array at all
The target, if present, must be to the right of `mid`, so `lo = mid + 1`
The target, if present, must be to the left of `mid`, so `hi = mid - 1`
You should restart the search from `lo = 0`
items.answer: The target, if present, must be to the right of `mid`, so `lo = mid + 1`
items.explanation_md: The array is sorted, so if `nums[mid]` is smaller than the target, every value at or before `mid` is also too small — the whole left half, including `mid`, can be discarded.

items.prompt_md: You're given a sorted array and must return the index of a target value in better than O(n) time. Which approach fits?
options: Scan left to right and stop at the first match
Compare against the midpoint and discard whichever half can't contain the target
Build a hash map from value to index, then look it up
Sort the array again and walk two pointers inward
items.answer: Compare against the midpoint and discard whichever half can't contain the target
items.explanation_md: Sorted data plus "find where a value sits" is the fingerprint for halving the search range each step — a hash map or a fresh sort would be unnecessary work the sortedness already buys you.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of binary search over a sorted array of length n?
items.explanation_md: Each comparison discards half of the remaining candidates, so the range shrinks to empty after about log2(n) steps.
